items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’m again plagued
by vague doubts...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the envoy has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Key comedic mechanism is the mock-heroic/highbrow phrase «терзают смутные сомнения» applied to petty theft logic, plus the parallel structure «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» meaning each LOST an item (not possession). The actual translation preserves the high-register “plagued by vague doubts” well, but mistranslates the second clause as possession (“has”), which breaks the intended inference chain (Shpak’s tape recorder is missing; the envoy’s medallion is missing) and weakens the joke/callback logic.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very nice, Tsar!
Very nice, hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very nice!
Hello, Tsar! Very nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The humor is Bunsha’s anachronistic, repetitive Soviet-style politeness while impersonating the Tsar, with the absurd insistence on “tsar” as if it were a name/title in a formal introduction. The translation keeps the repetition and the banal politeness (“Very nice… hello…”) and repeatedly foregrounds “Tsar,” so the comic effect largely survives. Slight loss: it doesn’t explicitly convey the self-introduction nuance (“I’m the Tsar”) as clearly as the reference, but the repeated address still signals it.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Matches the reference essentially verbatim in force, register, and comic authoritarian posture. No loss of meaning or tone.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the reference; preserves the archaic/high-register melancholy and the comic mismatch with the modern setting.

items.translation:
They bricked me in!
Bricked me in, demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Core joke is the Tsar’s mystical interpretation of a mundane mechanical trap; “demons” as agents is preserved. “Bricked me in” is a workable idiom for being walled up, though it’s a bit more specific/material than “walled me in” and loses the ellipsis-driven lament rhythm. Still comprehensible and funny.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Meaning is accurate (crediting the cross with the ‘miracle’). The reference’s “Behold the power…” better captures the elevated, proclamatory archaic flourish; the actual is slightly flatter/modern (“That’s what … can do”), reducing the mock-solemnity a bit.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The line is a punchy MC-style command; the reference opts for a slightly more ‘announcer’ phrasing that can feel period-appropriate and comic. “Everybody dance!” is idiomatic and keeps the imperative energy, but it narrows the meaning from “everyone dances”/“all are dancing” to a direct instruction and loses some of the ‘official announcement’ flavor. Still works on screen.

items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: The line is a breezy, self-satisfied burglar catchphrase: he’s pleased with his opportunistic entry. “What perfect timing!” conveys the satisfaction and situational luck, though it slightly shifts from “I dropped in well” (self-credit) to a more neutral timing comment. Comic intent largely preserved.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
Imported caviar—eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The key is the Soviet-food-shortage satire: “eggplant caviar” is not real caviar; calling it “caviar” in English risks confusion, so the reference avoids that by using “overseas spread.” The actual translation keeps “caviar” and only appends “eggplant,” which can mislead non-Russian viewers into thinking it’s a genuine caviar variety, blunting the joke’s inversion of status symbols.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The humor depends on Shpak’s escalating inventory list and the telltale hesitation where he initially says “a suede jacket… three,” revealing he’s inflating counts (and the comedic beat is the delayed “three”). The actual translation smooths this into a clean, consistent “three suede jackets,” removing the hesitation/beat and the self-betrayal. It also changes singular “куртка… замшевая… три” into an already-resolved plural, which weakens the comic effect even if the factual count ends up the same.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Captures the repeated structure and resigned comic rhythm; essentially matches the reference. Minor punctuation/line-break differences don’t harm effect.

summary.interpretation: 2.73 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.55 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
